votes.reason: The Python code correctly implements the transformation rules and iteratively calculates the sum, which is equivalent to the recursive Coq implementation.
The Python code correctly implements the Coq logic, including the index-based transformations and the sum calculation.
The Python code faithfully implements the transformation logic and the summation logic (iteratively vs recursively) defined in the Coq code.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification. transform_entry checks idx % 3 == 0 first (squaring), then idx % 4 == 0 (cubing), else returns num unchanged, matching the Coq logic. sum_squares_helper iteratively computes the same sum as the Coq recursive version, starting from the given index and incrementing for each element. sum_squares_spec checks if result equals sum_squares_helper(lst, 0), matching the Coq Prop definition. All concrete inputs will produce the same results.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification. transform_entry uses the same logic (idx % 3 == 0 for squaring, else idx % 4 == 0 for cubing, else identity). sum_squares_helper iteratively computes the same sum as the Coq recursive version, starting from the given index and incrementing. sum_squares_spec checks equality with sum_squares_helper starting at index 0, matching the Coq Prop definition for concrete result checking.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification. transform_entry checks idx % 3 == 0 first (returning num^2), then idx % 4 == 0 (returning num^3), otherwise returns num, matching the Coq logic. sum_squares_helper iteratively computes the same sum as the Coq recursive version, starting from the given index. sum_squares_spec correctly checks if result equals sum_squares_helper(lst, 0), matching the Coq Prop definition for concrete inputs.

Python matches Coq on all relevant inputs: same transform rules (priority to idx%3==0 over idx%4==0), same iterative sum with starting index 0, and the spec checks equality to the helper’s result. Indices used are nonnegative, so modulo behavior aligns.
Python matches Coq: same index-based transform (check idx%3 first, then idx%4), iterates with idx starting at 0, and compares result equality. Uses unbounded integers like Coq Z.
Python mirrors Coq: same divisibility-based transform on indices, same accumulation over the list starting at index 0, and the spec checks equality with the helper result.
